sections.title: Simple Future Endings
sections.explanation: The Spanish simple future tense is remarkably easy: you attach endings directly to the full infinitive, without removing anything. The same six endings apply to ALL verbs (-AR, -ER, and -IR): yo -é, tú -ás, él/ella/usted -á, nosotros -emos, vosotros -éis, ellos/ellas/ustedes -án. For example, hablar (to speak) → yo hablaré, tú hablarás, él hablará, nosotros hablaremos, vosotros hablaréis, ellos hablarán. The same pattern works for -ER verbs: comer → comeré, comerás, comerá, comeremos, comeréis, comerán. And for -IR verbs: vivir → viviré, vivirás, vivirá, viviremos, viviréis, vivirán. Note that all forms except nosotros carry a written accent.
sections.tip: Unlike the present tense, you never drop the infinitive ending in the simple future — just attach -é, -ás, -á, -emos, -éis, or -án directly to the full infinitive. Only nosotros (-emos) lacks a written accent.

examples.en: Tomorrow I will speak with my boss.
Will you eat with us tonight?

examples.es: Ella vivirá en París el año que viene.
examples.en: She will live in Paris next year.

examples.es: Vosotros beberéis agua en la carrera.
examples.en: You all will drink water in the race.

exercises.prompt: What is the correct simple future form of 'hablar' (to speak) for 'yo'?
exercises.explanation: Attach -é directly to the infinitive: hablar + é = hablaré. The written accent marks this as the future tense yo form.

exercises.prompt: Tú ___ (comer) pizza esta noche. (You will eat pizza tonight.)
exercises.hint: Attach the tú future ending -ás to the full infinitive comer
exercises.explanation: comer + ás = comerás. The tú form carries a written accent on the á.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'She will live in Madrid' in Spanish?
exercises.explanation: The él/ella future ending is -á: vivir + á = vivirá. The stem is the complete infinitive, nothing is removed.

exercises.prompt: Nosotros ___ (trabajar) mañana. (We will work tomorrow.)
exercises.hint: Attach -emos to the full infinitive trabajar — nosotros is the only future form without a written accent
exercises.explanation: trabajar + emos = trabajaremos. The nosotros form is the only one without a written accent in the simple future.

exercises.prompt: Which option correctly conjugates 'beber' (to drink) for 'ellos'?
exercises.explanation: The ellos/ellas future ending is -án: beber + án = beberán. Note: bebieron is the preterite (past) form, not future.

exercises.prompt: Vosotros ___ (escribir) los informes. (You all will write the reports.)
exercises.hint: Attach -éis to the full infinitive escribir
exercises.explanation: escribir + éis = escribiréis. The vosotros future ending -éis carries a written accent on the é.

exercises.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses the simple future tense?
options: El tren llegará a las tres.
El tren llegan a las tres.
El tren llegaré a las tres.
exercises.explanation: El tren (the train) is third person singular, so the correct ending is -á: llegar + á = llegará. Option B uses the yo ending and option C uses the present tense.

exercises.prompt: Usted ___ (hablar) con el director. (You will speak with the director.)
exercises.hint: Usted (formal you) uses the same ending as él/ella: -á
exercises.explanation: hablar + á = hablará. Usted always uses the third person singular (él/ella) form in all tenses.

sections.title: Irregular Future Stems
sections.explanation: A small group of common verbs use a modified stem in the future tense instead of the full infinitive. The endings remain exactly the same (-é, -ás, -á, -emos, -éis, -án), but the stem changes. The most important irregular stems are: tener (to have) → tendr-, poder (to be able to) → podr-, hacer (to do/make) → har-, venir (to come) → vendr-, salir (to leave/go out) → saldr-, saber (to know) → sabr-. Examples: tener → yo tendré, tú tendrás, él tendrá, nosotros tendremos, vosotros tendréis, ellos tendrán. Hacer → yo haré, tú harás, él hará. Notice that hacer drops the -ce entirely. Poder → yo podré, tú podrás. Saber → yo sabré. These verbs are all high-frequency, so it is worth memorizing their stems.
sections.tip: Several irregular stems follow a pattern: the vowel of the infinitive ending is replaced by a -d- (tener→tendr, venir→vendr, salir→saldr, poder→podr). Hacer is the most unusual — it shortens dramatically to har-. Saber shortens to sabr-.

examples.es: No podré ir a la fiesta.
examples.en: I won't be able to go to the party.
What will you do this weekend?
The plane will leave on time.

exercises.prompt: What is the correct future form of 'tener' (to have) for 'yo'?
exercises.explanation: tener has the irregular stem tendr-. Add the yo ending -é: tendr + é = tendré. Never attach the ending to the full infinitive for irregular verbs.

exercises.prompt: Ella ___ (poder) hablar contigo después. (She will be able to talk to you later.)
exercises.hint: The irregular stem of poder is podr-; use the ella ending -á
exercises.explanation: poder → irregular stem podr- + á = podrá. The vowel of -er is dropped and replaced with nothing (not a -d-).

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'We will do the work' using 'hacer'?
exercises.explanation: hacer has the highly irregular stem har-. Add nosotros ending -emos: har + emos = haremos. The -ce of hacer is completely dropped.

exercises.prompt: ¿A qué hora ___ (venir) tus amigos? (What time will your friends come?)
exercises.hint: The irregular stem of venir is vendr-; use the ellos/ellas ending -án
exercises.explanation: venir → irregular stem vendr- + án = vendrán. The pattern -dr- replaces the -ir ending of the infinitive.

exercises.prompt: Which is the correct future tense of 'salir' (to leave) for 'tú'?
exercises.explanation: salir → irregular stem saldr-. Add tú ending -ás: saldr + ás = saldrás. The -ir ending becomes -dr-.

exercises.prompt: Yo ___ (saber) los resultados mañana. (I will know the results tomorrow.)
exercises.hint: The irregular stem of saber is sabr-; use the yo ending -é
exercises.explanation: saber → irregular stem sabr- + é = sabré. The vowel -e of the infinitive ending is simply dropped.

exercises.prompt: Your friend asks: '¿Cuándo ___ (venir) tú a verme?' Which option correctly fills the blank?
exercises.explanation: venir → irregular stem vendr- + tú ending -ás = vendrás. Only the endings from the standard set are used, even with irregular stems.

exercises.prompt: Which of these future forms is INCORRECT?
exercises.explanation: tener uses the irregular stem tendr-, not the full infinitive. The correct form is ella tendrá, not tenerá. The other two forms (hará, saldremos) correctly use their irregular stems.

sections.title: ir a + Infinitive (Near Future)
sections.explanation: Spanish has a very common alternative way to talk about the future: ir a + infinitive. This construction mirrors the English 'going to' and expresses actions that are planned or about to happen in the near future. The formula is: conjugated form of ir (in the present tense) + a + infinitive. The present tense of ir: yo voy, tú vas, él/ella va, nosotros vamos, vosotros vais, ellos/ellas van. Examples: voy a comer (I am going to eat / I will eat), vas a llamar (you are going to call), va a llover (it is going to rain), vamos a estudiar (we are going to study). While the simple future (comeré) and ir a + infinitive (voy a comer) can often be used interchangeably, ir a + infinitive usually implies a more immediate or concrete plan, while the simple future can sound more formal or refer to events further in the future.
sections.tip: Think of 'ir a + infinitive' as the Spanish equivalent of English 'going to'. It is widely used in everyday conversation and is often preferred over the simple future for near-term plans. Remember: it is always 'ir a' — never 'ir de' or 'ir para' in this construction.

examples.es: Voy a comer una pizza esta noche.
examples.en: I am going to eat a pizza tonight.
Are you going to call your mother?
It is going to rain this afternoon.

examples.es: Vamos a estudiar para el examen.
examples.en: We are going to study for the exam.
Are you all going to go to the concert?

examples.es: Van a abrir un restaurante nuevo.
examples.en: They are going to open a new restaurant.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'I am going to eat' using 'ir a + infinitive'?
exercises.explanation: The structure is ir (conjugated) + a + infinitive. 'Voy' is the yo form of ir; you must include the preposition 'a' before the infinitive: voy a comer.

exercises.prompt: Ella ___ ___ ___ (ir a + llamar) a sus padres. (She is going to call her parents.)
exercises.hint: Use the ella form of ir in the present tense, followed by 'a' and the infinitive llamar
exercises.explanation: ella va + a + llamar = va a llamar. The present tense of ir for ella is 'va'.

exercises.prompt: Which sentence means 'We are going to travel to Mexico'?
exercises.explanation: The nosotros form of ir is 'vamos'. The structure requires 'a' before the infinitive: vamos a viajar. Option C doubles up constructions incorrectly.

exercises.prompt: ¿___ ___ ___ (ir a + hacer) tú esta tarde? (What are you going to do this afternoon?)
exercises.hint: Use the tú form of ir in the present tense, then 'a', then hacer
exercises.explanation: tú vas + a + hacer = vas a hacer. The tú present form of ir is 'vas'.

exercises.prompt: Choose the sentence that correctly expresses a near-future plan using 'ir a + infinitive':
options: Van a abrir una tienda nueva.
Vanen a abrir una tienda nueva.
exercises.explanation: 'Van' is the ellos/ellas/ustedes form of ir. The preposition 'a' is required: van a abrir. 'Vanen' does not exist in Spanish.

exercises.prompt: How do 'voy a comer' and 'comeré' differ?
options: They cannot be used interchangeably and have entirely different meanings.
'Voy a comer' implies a near-term plan; 'comeré' sounds more formal or refers to a less immediate future.
'Voy a comer' is past tense; 'comeré' is present tense.
exercises.explanation: Both mean 'I will eat', but 'voy a comer' (ir a + infinitive) typically conveys an imminent, concrete plan, while 'comeré' (simple future) can sound more formal or distant. In everyday speech, 'voy a comer' is more common.

exercises.prompt: Nosotros ___ ___ ___ (ir a + aprender) francés. (We are going to learn French.)
exercises.hint: The nosotros form of ir in the present tense is 'vamos'
exercises.explanation: nosotros vamos + a + aprender = vamos a aprender. 'Vamos a + infinitive' is one of the most common future constructions in spoken Spanish.

exercises.prompt: Translate to Spanish using 'ir a + infinitive': 'Are you all going to watch the game?'
exercises.explanation: 'You all' maps to vosotros, whose form of ir is 'vais'. Add 'a' before the infinitive ver: ¿vais a ver el partido? Option C uses the tú form (vas), which is singular.
